@@ -1208,6 +1208,7 @@
 {"id":"bd-vql1","title":"External journal: wire replay through external device ByteDevice","description":"Wire the external journal replay path:
1. Open the external journal image as a FileByteDevice.
2. Read journal blocks from the external device.
3. Feed them to replay_jbd2_segments (existing function) with the DATA device as the write target.
4. After successful replay, clear needs_recovery on the data device.

The key insight: journal blocks are READ from the external device, but replayed blocks are WRITTEN to the data device. The existing replay infrastructure accepts a BlockDevice for writes — just pass the data device's block adapter.

DEPENDS ON: Journal segment computation (bd-vdk6).

ACCEPTANCE: A test with a synthetic data FS + dirty external journal verifies that replay writes land on the data device and needs_recovery is cleared.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:08:52.815573813Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-25T03:41:58.713180412Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T03:41:58.712703279Z","close_reason":"Already implemented by concurrent agent: ExternalJournalReplayAdapter reads journal blocks from external device, writes replayed blocks to data device. UUID/block-size validation, JBD2 replay, superblock re-read after replay. Test open_fs_replays_external_journal_transaction passes.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ext4","journal","journal_dev"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-vql1","depends_on_id":"bd-vdk6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:09:02.571799150Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-vql1","depends_on_id":"bd-xvoe","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:11:35.150342179Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-vs8d1","title":"port ext4 quota inode superblock parity","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T00:34:13.301096338Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-21T00:52:56.159126992Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T00:52:56.156670520Z","close_reason":"Implemented ext4 quota inode superblock parsing and feature-aware accessors","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-vsv9","title":"Add btrfs send-stream reference dump harness","description":"Extend the btrfs reference harness to generate CRC-valid send streams, compare FrankenFS parse output against upstream btrfs receive --dump normalization, and tighten FEATURE_PARITY evidence for send-stream compatibility.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"ubuntu","created_at":"2026-04-16T22:36:47.057142215Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-16T22:42:33.672391975Z","closed_at":"2026-04-16T22:42:33.669523634Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["conformance","testing"]}
+{"id":"bd-vwzei","title":"[FUSE] FITRIM ioctl — fstrim(8) compat / discard accounting","description":"FITRIM (_IOWR(X, 121, struct fstrim_range) = 0xc0185879) is the ioctl fstrim(8) issues to a mounted FS to release freed blocks back to the underlying block device. struct fstrim_range is 24 bytes: u64 start + u64 len + u64 minlen. On success the kernel rewrites len with the actual bytes discarded so userspace can report a number. Most modern SSDs do TRIM in the device driver, so a userspace FUSE FS that has no direct discard path SHOULD: validate the range vs the FS size, accept any minlen, and return len=0 (zero bytes discarded but call succeeded) — matches Linux kernel behavior on filesystems mounted on non-discard-capable devices. Required: dispatch_ioctl branch on FITRIM that parses the 24-byte input, validates start within FS extent, returns the modified 24-byte struct with bytes_discarded=0. Acceptance: ioctl(fd, FITRIM, &range) on a regular file returns the struct echoed back with len=0; rejects malformed buffers (<24 bytes) with EINVAL; rejects start past EOF of the device with EINVAL.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-25T20:15:40.577593345Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-25T20:22:58.680796819Z","closed_at":"2026-04-25T20:22:58.678891224Z","close_reason":"FITRIM shipped via FsOps::trim_range + OpenFs override (validate + saturate + return 0 since the BlockDevice has no discard) + dispatch_ioctl branch + parse_fstrim_range / encode_fstrim_response. 6 regressions cover encoded round-trip, EINVAL on short input + start past EOF, EROFS read-only, len=u64::MAX saturation, 0 bytes discarded on default fixture.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-vyt6","title":"Add current-engine terminology erratum to comprehensive spec and fix ext4-only top-line wording","description":"COMPREHENSIVE_SPEC_FOR_FRANKENFS_V1.md still opens with ext4-only wording and repeatedly uses FrankenFsEngine nomenclature that can be misread as the active FUSE runtime path. Add a focused errata bullet in §0.1.1 clarifying current code-path mapping () and adjust the top-line intro sentence to ext4+btrfs wording. Keep changes minimal and avoid broad spec rewrites.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-26T01:44:56.210135235Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-26T01:45:18.135506890Z","closed_at":"2026-02-26T01:45:18.135487854Z","close_reason":"Completed focused spec errata note for engine terminology and ext4+btrfs intro wording","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["docs","spec"]}
 {"id":"bd-w0pr","title":"Wire repair refresh lifecycle into OpenFs write path","description":"Spec §12.1.3 requires marking groups dirty + queued refresh after writes; ffs-repair QueuedRepairRefresh exists but ffs-core/ffs-block write path doesn't wire RepairFlushLifecycle. Integrate ArcCache with QueuedRepairRefresh + ScrubDaemon pipeline or equivalent.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-13T18:55:03.478585707Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-15T00:39:42.328256059Z","closed_at":"2026-04-15T00:39:42.324578806Z","close_reason":"Wired RepairFlushLifecycle into OpenFs commit paths. commit_transaction, commit_transaction_ssi, and commit_transaction_journaled now notify the repair lifecycle with committed block numbers, allowing repair subsystem to queue symbol refresh per spec §12.1.3.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-w1n6b","title":"[FUZZ-TARGET] add ffs-fuse ioctl argument harness","description":"Apply /testing-fuzzing to the `ffs-fuse` ioctl dispatch boundary. Build a fuzz harness around `dispatch_ioctl(...)` / `dispatch_ioctl_for_testing(...)` that mutates `cmd`, `in_data`, `out_size`, `fh`, `caller_pid`, and read-only/writeable mount options while using the existing recorder/test doubles. The goal is to stress the argument parsers and ioctl response encoding without needing a live mount.

Primary targets:
- `crates/ffs-fuse/src/lib.rs:1213-1353` FIEMAP parsing/encoding helpers
- `crates/ffs-fuse/src/lib.rs:1241-1300` move_ext / u32 / flag parsers
- `crates/ffs-fuse/src/lib.rs:1536-1833` ioctl dispatch for FIEMAP, GET/SETFLAGS, GET/SETVERSION, fscrypt, move_ext, fslabel, and btrfs ioctl cases

Mutation classes:
- short/oversized ioctl buffers
- inconsistent `out_size` vs payload sizes
- garbage command numbers / incompatible flag bits
- malformed fscrypt `_EX`, move_ext, FIEMAP, and fslabel argument layouts

Acceptance:
- new fuzz target added under `fuzz/fuzz_targets/` and registered in `fuzz/Cargo.toml`
- harness proves no panic across malformed ioctl argument shapes
- any crashers become regression tests next to the current unit coverage","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-22T08:34:30Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-22T09:47:19Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T09:47:19Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["FUZZ-TARGET","ffs-fuse","fuzzing","ioctl","mount-path","testing"]}
